items.q: What is the difference between species richness and species evenness?
items.correct: Richness counts how many species exist; evenness measures how balanced their abundances are.
items.random_wrong: Richness is for plants, evenness for animals.
items.why: Students conflate these. A community with 10 species each having 100 individuals has high evenness; one with 10 species but 991 of species A and 1 of each other has low evenness despite identical richness.

items.correct: Dissolved CO2 forms carbonic acid, lowering ocean pH.
items.misconception: Acid rain falling on the ocean.
items.why: Students think acidification is direct pollution. The main driver is atmospheric CO2 dissolving in seawater - an inevitable result of higher atmospheric concentrations, threatening shell-forming organisms.

items.q: How does fertiliser runoff cause aquatic dead zones?
items.correct: Excess nutrients fuel algal blooms; when algae die, decomposition depletes water oxygen.
items.random_wrong: Fertiliser makes the water too acidic.
items.why: Students assume direct toxicity. Eutrophication is INDIRECT - nutrients cause excess plant growth, then decomposers consume oxygen breaking it down, suffocating fish and other aerobic organisms.

items.q: Hardin's 'Tragedy of the Commons' was published in:
items.why: Correct answer: 1968. “1962” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The Brundtland definition of sustainable development appeared in:
items.why: It's 1987. “1972” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Year of the Rio Earth Summit?
items.why: The real figure is 1992 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
